title: Error State
description: Error State communicates that a task or view failed and helps the user recover through clear explanation, optional iconography, actions, and custom follow-up content.
intro: Use `ui-error-state` for failed loads, missing resources, permission blocks, and similar broken flows where the user needs both context and a next step. The copy should explain the problem in human terms, not just restate a technical failure.

featureSections.title: Basic error messaging
featureSections.description: The simplest pattern is a short title and one sentence explaining what failed. This covers many general-purpose error states.

featureSections.title: Icons and sizes
featureSections.description: Different error contexts may need a more specific icon or a different visual scale depending on whether the state appears in a compact panel or a larger surface.

featureSections.title: Recovery actions
featureSections.description: Actions are useful when the user can retry, request help, or follow a concrete recovery path instead of being left at a dead end.

featureSections.title: Custom projected guidance
featureSections.description: Projected content helps when the user needs more than one sentence, such as validation guidance or a short list of issues to resolve.

featureSections.title: Common error scenarios
featureSections.description: Network issues, permission failures, and missing resources are not the same problem and should not share the same copy or recovery expectations.

featureSections.title: Embedded panel or view layout
featureSections.description: Error states usually appear inside larger shells such as results panels, dashboards, or activity views, not as isolated blank canvases.

accessibility.title: Clear problem statement
accessibility.body: An error state should clearly describe what failed and, when possible, what the user can do next. Avoid vague titles that communicate only that 'something happened.'

accessibility.title: Recovery actions
accessibility.body:
If actions are present, they should offer meaningful recovery rather than decorative options.

| Element | Guidance |
| --- | --- |
| Primary action | Use for the most direct recovery path, such as retrying. |
| Secondary action | Use for alternate paths such as contacting support or going elsewhere. |
| Custom projected controls | Keep them simple and semantically clear. |

accessibility.title: Icons and supporting content
accessibility.body: Icons and custom projected details should reinforce the error message, not replace it. The main title and description must still communicate the state clearly on their own.

apiSections.title: ErrorStateComponent - ui-error-state
apiSections.lead: Standalone component for presenting failed states with concise messaging, optional iconography, optional recovery actions, and projected custom content.

blocks.title: Inputs
rows: Contextual icon used for the error state.
Overall scale of the error state.

blocks.title: Projected content
rows: Optional projected custom content rendered under the main message.
{ label, variant?, appearance?, icon?, size?, disabled?, action? }
Action contract used by primary and secondary buttons.

paragraphs: Tailor the wording to the kind of failure. Retryable system errors, missing permissions, and missing resources should not sound the same.
Use projected custom content sparingly. If the state turns into a dense troubleshooting screen, the surrounding flow likely needs a different design.
blocks.title: Notes
